subtopics.title: Introduction to Error Spotting & Sentence Improvement

blocks.title: What is Error Spotting?
blocks.content: Error spotting questions test your ability to identify grammatical, syntactical, or vocabulary errors in a sentence. You must recognise incorrect usage and choose the correct option.

blocks.title: What is Sentence Improvement?
blocks.content: Sentence improvement questions ask you to replace an underlined or highlighted part of a sentence with a better option, or choose the best version from given alternatives.

blocks.title: Step-by-Step Approach for Error Spotting
steps: Step 1: Read the entire sentence carefully.
Step 2: Identify the subject and verb – check agreement.
Step 3: Check the tense – does it match the context?
Step 4: Check prepositions, articles, and pronouns.
Step 5: Look for parallelism and modifier placement.
Step 6: Check for redundancy or word choice errors.
Step 7: If the sentence sounds wrong, it usually is.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Error spotting requires a strong grasp of grammar rules.
Sentence improvement tests your ability to choose the best expression.
Always read the full sentence before deciding on errors.
Trust your ear – if something sounds off, it likely is an error.

blocks.title: Core Rule
blocks.content: A singular subject takes a singular verb; a plural subject takes a plural verb. Errors arise when the subject and verb are separated by intervening phrases or when the subject is not obvious.

blocks.title: Common Subject-Verb Agreement Errors
rows: Collective nouns (team, group, committee) take singular verbs.
Each, every, either, neither are singular.
Each of the students are responsible.
Each of the students is responsible.
Intervening phrases do NOT change the subject.
The box of chocolates are on the table.
The box of chocolates is on the table.
The teacher and the student is coming.
The teacher and the student are coming.
Subjects joined by 'or/nor' agree with the nearest subject.
Neither the teacher nor the students is here.
Neither the teacher nor the students are here.
Indefinite pronouns (everyone, somebody, anyone) are singular.

blocks.title: Example 1: Collective Noun
given: The jury have reached a verdict.
steps: 'Jury' is a collective noun.
Collective nouns take singular verbs when acting as a unit.
The jury has reached a verdict.

blocks.title: Example 2: Intervening Phrase
given: The list of requirements are too long.
steps: Subject is 'list' (singular), not 'requirements'.
The phrase 'of requirements' is an intervening phrase.
The list of requirements is too long.

blocks.title: Trap: Ignoring the true subject.
blocks.wrong: Choosing the verb based on the noun closest to it.
blocks.correct: Identify the true subject before choosing the verb.
blocks.tip: Look beyond intervening phrases and clauses.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Collective nouns → singular verbs.
Each/Either/Neither → singular.
Intervening phrases don't change the subject.
Subjects with 'and' → plural.
Subjects with 'or/nor' → agree with nearest.
Indefinite pronouns → singular.
Uncountable nouns → singular.

blocks.title: Common Tense Errors
rows: The train left before I arrived.
The train had left before I arrived.

blocks.title: Example: Present with Past Marker
given: She has completed the project last week.
steps: 'Last week' is a past time marker.
Present Perfect ('has completed') cannot be used with past time markers.
She completed the project last week.
blocks.formula_used: Past Indefinite with past time markers

blocks.title: Example: Past Perfect with Two Past Actions
given: The movie started before we reached the theatre.
steps: Two past actions: 'started' and 'reached'.
The movie started first, so it should be Past Perfect.
The movie had started before we reached the theatre.

blocks.title: Trap: Using Present Perfect with specific past time.
blocks.tip: Past time markers (yesterday, last week, in 2010) require Past Indefinite.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Past time markers → Past Indefinite.
Since/for → Present Perfect.
Two past actions → Past Perfect for the earlier one.
Future in past → would.
Stative verbs → no continuous form.

blocks.title: Common Preposition Errors
rows: I have been here since two hours.
I have been here for two hours.
I will meet you on 5 pm.
I will meet you at 5 pm.
Distribute this between the team members.
Distribute this among the team members.
I have other books beside this.
I have other books besides this.

blocks.title: Example: Since vs For
given: She has been working here since 5 years.
steps: '5 years' is a duration, not a point in time.
Use 'for' with duration, 'since' with a point in time.
She has been working here for 5 years.
blocks.formula_used: Since = point in time, For = duration

blocks.title: Example: Between vs Among
given: The property was divided between the three brothers.
steps: 'Between' is used for two; 'Among' is used for more than two.
There are three brothers, so 'among' is correct.
The property was divided among the three brothers.
blocks.formula_used: Between = two; Among = more than two

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing 'In' and 'Into'.
blocks.tip: 'In' indicates position; 'Into' indicates movement toward the inside.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Since = point in time, For = duration.
In = position, Into = movement.
At = specific time/point, On = day/surface, In = month/year/enclosed.
Between = two, Among = more than two.
Beside = next to, Besides = in addition to.

subtopics.title: Article Errors – A, An, The, or No Article

blocks.title: Common Article Errors
rows: The sun rises in the east.
The honesty is the best policy.

blocks.title: Example: 'a' vs 'an'
steps: 'Honest' starts with a vowel sound (o).
Use 'an' before vowel sounds.
She is an honest woman.
blocks.formula_used: a/an = based on sound, not spelling

blocks.title: Example: Missing 'the' with Superlative
given: Mount Everest is highest peak in the world.
steps: Superlatives require 'the'.
Mount Everest is the highest peak in the world.

blocks.title: Trap: Using 'the' with abstract nouns in general sense.
blocks.tip: Abstract nouns (honesty, happiness, truth) in general sense take no article.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: A/An = based on sound (vowel/consonant).
The = specific/unique/superlative.
No article = plural general, abstract nouns.
First mention = a/an; second mention = the.

blocks.title: Common Pronoun Errors
rows: Me and him went to the store.
He and I went to the store.
Everyone should bring their own lunch.
Everyone should bring his or her own lunch.
He told John that he was wrong.
He told John that John was wrong.

blocks.title: Example: Subjective vs Objective Case
given: Me and my friend are going to the party.
steps: The pronoun is the subject of the sentence.
Subjective case should be used: 'I' not 'me'.
My friend and I are going to the party.

blocks.title: Example: Ambiguous Reference
given: Rita told Priya that she was late.
steps: 'She' could refer to Rita or Priya – ambiguous.
Rewrite to clarify: Rita told Priya that Priya was late.
Or: Rita told Priya that she herself was late.
blocks.answer: Rita told Priya that Priya was late.

blocks.title: Trap: Using objective case for the subject.
blocks.tip: Always use subjective case (I, he, she, we, they) for subjects.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Subjective case (I, he, she, we, they) = subject.
Objective case (me, him, her, us, them) = object.
Pronouns must agree with their antecedents.
Avoid ambiguous pronoun references.
Who = subject; Whom = object.

blocks.title: Modifier Errors
blocks.subtitle: Words That Need to Be Close to What They Modify

blocks.title: Types of Modifier Errors
blocks.content:
• Dangling: The modifier doesn't have a clear subject to modify.
• Misplaced: The modifier is too far from the word it modifies.
• Squinting: The modifier could refer to either of two words.

blocks.title: Common Modifier Errors
rows: Walking down the street, the flowers looked beautiful.
Walking down the street, I thought the flowers looked beautiful.
He almost drove his car for an hour.
He drove his car for almost an hour.
Students who study rarely get good grades.
Students who rarely study get good grades.

blocks.title: Example: Dangling Modifier
given: Running fast, the finish line appeared.
steps: Who was running fast? The finish line wasn't running.
Add a clear subject: Running fast, the athlete reached the finish line.
Or: The athlete, running fast, reached the finish line.
blocks.answer: Running fast, the athlete reached the finish line.
blocks.formula_used: Modifier must have a clear subject

blocks.title: Example: Misplaced Modifier
given: He almost ate the entire pizza.
steps: 'Almost' modifies 'ate' – he almost ate = he didn't eat.
But the intended meaning is he ate almost the entire pizza.
He ate almost the entire pizza.
blocks.answer: He ate almost the entire pizza.
blocks.formula_used: Place modifiers close to what they modify

blocks.title: Trap: Misplacing 'only', 'almost', 'just'.
blocks.tip: Place 'only' before the word it modifies.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Dangling: Missing subject for the modifier.
Misplaced: Modifier too far from what it modifies.
Squinting: Modifier could refer to two things.
Place modifiers close to the words they modify.

blocks.title: What is Parallelism?
blocks.content: Parallelism is the use of similar grammatical structures for similar ideas. Errors occur when the items in a list or comparison are not in the same form.

blocks.title: Common Parallelism Errors
rows: She likes swimming, running, and to bike.
She likes swimming, running, and biking.
He is better at math than his brother is.
He is better at math than his brother.
He is not only smart but also he is kind.
He is not only smart but also kind.
She is either studying or she is working.
She is either studying or working.

blocks.title: Example: List of Items
given: I enjoy reading books, listening to music, and to travel.
steps: The list uses 'reading' and 'listening' (gerunds).
'To travel' breaks the pattern – should be 'travelling'.
I enjoy reading books, listening to music, and travelling.

blocks.title: Example: Not Only...But Also
given: She is not only intelligent but also she is hardworking.
steps: After 'but also', we expect the same grammatical form.
'Intelligent' is an adjective; so 'hardworking' should be an adjective.
She is not only intelligent but also hardworking.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Lists must have consistent grammatical forms.
Comparisons must compare like items.
Correlative conjunctions (not only...but also, either...or) require parallel structure.
Parallel structure makes sentences clear and balanced.

blocks.title: Common Redundancy Errors
rows: The government, they passed the law.

blocks.title: Example: Redundancy
given: The reason for his absence is because he is sick.
steps: 'The reason is' already implies a cause.
'Because' repeats the idea of causation.
The reason for his absence is that he is sick.
blocks.answer: The reason for his absence is that he is sick.

blocks.title: Example: Unnecessary Words
given: He returned back to the office.
steps: 'Returned' already means 'came back'.
'Back' is redundant.
He returned to the office.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Avoid 'reason...because' → use 'reason...that'.
Avoid 'return back' → use 'return'.
Avoid 'final conclusion' → use 'conclusion'.
Avoid 'same identical' → use 'same'.
Concise writing is preferred in exams.

blocks.title: Common Conjunction Errors
rows: Because he was sick, he went to work.
Although he was sick, he went to work.
Inspite of the rain, the match continued.
In spite of the rain, the match continued.
You cannot pass unless you do not study.
You cannot pass unless you study.
He is so tired that he cannot walk.
He is too tired to walk.

blocks.title: Example: Because vs Although
given: Because he worked hard, he failed the exam.
steps: Working hard and failing doesn't make logical sense.
The correct conjunction should be 'Although'.
Although he worked hard, he failed the exam.
blocks.formula_used: Choose conjunction that fits the logical relationship

blocks.title: Example: Double Negative with Unless
given: You cannot succeed unless you do not work hard.
steps: 'Unless' already means 'if not'.
Adding another negative creates a positive meaning.
You cannot succeed unless you work hard.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Because = reason; Although = contrast.
Despite/In spite of = contrast (followed by noun/gerund).
Unless = if not (don't add another negative).
So...that = result; Too...to = excess preventing action.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategies for Error Spotting & Improvement

blocks.title: Strategy 1 – Read Aloud (in your head)
blocks.content: Reading the sentence silently in your mind helps you catch errors. If it sounds wrong, it probably is.

blocks.title: Strategy 2 – Split the Sentence
blocks.content: Break the sentence into clauses. Check each clause separately for grammar and logic.

blocks.title: Strategy 3 – Eliminate Options
blocks.content: In sentence improvement, eliminate options that are grammatically incorrect or wordy. The best option is often the simplest and clearest.

blocks.title: Strategy 4 – Check for 'No Error' Options
blocks.content: If the sentence sounds perfect, check all rules carefully. Sometimes 'No error' is the correct answer.

blocks.title: Memory Trick
blocks.memory_trick: Subject, Prepositions, Articles, Redundancy, Tense, Agreement – the six areas to check for errors.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Read sentences carefully and trust your ear.
Break sentences into clauses for analysis.
Eliminate options systematically.
Don't automatically assume there's an error – 'No error' is often correct.

blocks.title: Error Spotting – Practice Set
blocks.subtitle: Find the Error in Each Sentence

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Read the sentence carefully. Check each part for errors: subject-verb agreement, tense, preposition, article, pronoun, parallelism, and word choice.

blocks.question: Each of the students ______ responsible for their own work.
blocks.explanation: 'Each' is singular. The correct verb is 'is'.

blocks.question: She has been working here ______ 2015.
blocks.explanation: '2015' is a point in time → use 'since'.

blocks.question: The reason for his delay is ______ he missed the bus.
blocks.explanation: 'The reason is that' is the correct construction – not 'reason because'.

blocks.question: He is ______ honest man.
blocks.explanation: 'Honest' starts with a vowel sound (o) → use 'an'.

blocks.question: Between you and ______, this is a secret.
blocks.explanation: Objective case 'me' is used after the preposition 'between'.

blocks.question: He is better at math ______ his brother.
blocks.explanation: 'Than' is used for comparisons.

blocks.question: Neither the manager nor the employees ______ responsible for the error.
blocks.explanation: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the nearest subject ('employees' = plural) → 'are'.

blocks.question: She is not only smart ______ also hardworking.
blocks.explanation: The correct correlative pair is 'not only...but also'.

blocks.question: The committee ______ divided in their opinions.
blocks.explanation: Collective noun 'committee' takes singular verb if acting as a unit. However, 'in their opinions' indicates individual members, so plural is acceptable in this context. The correct answer is 'are' as the committee members are acting individually.

blocks.question: He ______ to the party yesterday.
blocks.explanation: 'Yesterday' indicates past → Past Indefinite 'went' is correct.

blocks.title: Post-Test Review – Error Spotting
blocks.content: Review subject-verb agreement, tense consistency, preposition usage, and article rules. These are the most frequently tested error types.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Read the sentence and identify the issue. Choose the option that makes the sentence grammatically correct, concise, and clear. The simplest correct option is often the best.

blocks.question: I have seen him ______.
blocks.explanation: The sentence uses Present Perfect 'have seen'. Present Perfect cannot be used with past time markers. All options are past time markers – but the question is about the entire improvement. Actually, the sentence should be changed to 'I saw him yesterday' if 'yesterday' is the option. The best improvement is to change the tense to Past Indefinite.

blocks.question: Which of the following is the best improvement for: 'He is more smarter than his brother.'
options: He is more smart than his brother.
He is smarter than his brother.
He is much smarter than his brother.
He is more smarter than his brother.
blocks.explanation: 'Smarter' is already comparative. 'More smarter' is double comparative and incorrect. The correct form is 'smarter than'.

blocks.question: Improve: 'She is the best and greatest dancer.'
options: She is the best and greatest dancer.
She is the best dancer and greatest.
blocks.explanation: 'Best' and 'greatest' are redundant – they mean almost the same. Choose one to avoid redundancy. The simplest version is 'She is the best dancer.'

blocks.question: Improve: 'Despite of the rain, the match continued.'
options: Despite the rain, the match continued.
In spite of the rain, the match continued.
Despite of the rain, the match continued.
blocks.explanation: 'Despite' does not take 'of'. The correct forms are 'Despite the rain' or 'In spite of the rain'.

blocks.question: Improve: 'The news are very good.'
blocks.explanation: 'News' is an uncountable noun and takes a singular verb → 'The news is very good.'

blocks.question: Improve: 'He returned back home.'
blocks.explanation: 'Returned' already means 'came back' – 'back' is redundant. The correct version is 'He returned home.'

blocks.question: Improve: 'I am very much tired.'
blocks.explanation: 'Much' is not used with 'very' to modify an adjective. 'Very tired' is the correct form.

blocks.question: Improve: 'He is one of those men who loves their country.'
options: He is one of those men who love their country.
He is one of those men who loves his country.
He is one of the men who loves their country.
He is one of those men who love his country.
blocks.explanation: The antecedent of 'who' is 'men' (plural), so the verb should be 'love' (plural). The pronoun should be 'their' to agree with 'men'.

blocks.question: Improve: 'He is the tallest of all the other students.'
options: He is the tallest of all the students.
He is the tallest of all the other students.
He is the tallest among all the other students.
He is taller than all the other students.
blocks.explanation: When comparing one person to others, use 'taller than all the other students'. 'Tallest of all' is used when comparing against a group that includes the person.

blocks.question: Improve: 'The Principal and the staff is present.'
options: The Principal and the staff are present.
The Principal and the staff is present.
The Principal and staff is present.
The Principal with the staff is present.
blocks.explanation: The subject is compound ('Principal and staff') – plural, so 'are' is correct.

blocks.title: Post-Test Review – Sentence Improvement
blocks.content: Focus on choosing the simplest, clearest, and most grammatically correct version. Avoid redundancy, double comparatives, and agreement errors.

blocks.title: Final Revision – Error Spotting & Sentence Improvement
points: Subject-Verb Agreement: Collective nouns, each/every, intervening phrases.
Tenses: Past time markers, Present Perfect, Past Perfect.
Prepositions: Since vs For, In vs Into, Between vs Among.
Articles: A/An based on sound, The for specific/superlative.
Pronouns: Case (subjective/objective), agreement, clear reference.
Modifiers: Avoid dangling, misplaced, and squinting modifiers.
Parallelism: Keep lists and comparisons parallel.
Redundancy: Remove unnecessary words.
Conjunctions: Use the correct logical connector.
Read aloud to catch errors.
Eliminate options systematically.
Trust your ear – if it sounds wrong, it probably is.
